title: Your First Engineering Design: Build It, Break It, Improve It
description: Complete a mini engineering design challenge using household materials. This session practices the design process, structural thinking, and iterative improvement.

steps.instruction: Design challenge: build the tallest freestanding tower you can from 10 index cards (or equivalent paper) and tape. Read about the engineering design process: what are the phases and why is defining requirements the first step?
steps.expectedOutcome: You can state the design requirements (tallest freestanding tower from given materials) and identify constraints (material limits) and resources (tape, cards)
steps.hint: A requirement is something the design MUST do; a constraint is something it CANNOT violate; resources are what you have available

steps.instruction: Before building, sketch 3 different tower designs. Consider: triangular base vs square base? Solid walls vs open frame? Read about structural loads and structural forms: why is the triangle the strongest shape?
steps.expectedOutcome: You can explain that triangles are rigid (cannot deform without changing side lengths) while squares can collapse, making triangular structures stronger
steps.hint: A triangular frame distributes load through tension and compression in members; a square frame can shear (collapse to a parallelogram)

steps.instruction: Build your first design. Test it: does it stand? How tall is it? Apply a gentle horizontal force. Read about failure modes: how did it fail (buckling, toppling, hinge formation)?
steps.expectedOutcome: You can identify and name the failure mode (e.g., buckling of thin walls, toppling due to narrow base) and explain why it occurred structurally
steps.hint: Tall thin structures buckle; narrow-base structures topple; weak joints hinge — each failure reveals a design improvement

steps.instruction: Rebuild with one improvement based on your failure analysis. Read about rapid prototyping: why is it better to build-test-improve quickly rather than planning the perfect design upfront?
steps.expectedOutcome: Your improved tower is taller or more stable, and you can articulate the specific change made and why it should address the observed failure mode
steps.hint: Rapid iteration reveals failures you couldn't predict — physical prototypes contain information that sketches miss

steps.instruction: Measure your final tower height and compare to classmates or your initial goal. Read about tradeoff analysis: what tradeoffs did you make between height, stability, and material usage?
steps.expectedOutcome: You can identify at least one design tradeoff (e.g., adding a wide base improves stability but uses material that could add height) and justify your final design choices
steps.hint: Every design decision involves tradeoffs — there is no free improvement. Width vs height, material per joint vs structural connections all trade off
